All right, your back got a great story for you. So remember it was 30 years ago, I shouldn't date this training, but that's when it was that Tuesday evening, standing in front of the Stratnites, the 12 ninkam poops who couldn't close the door, and I say, guys, you got four seconds to establish three crucial things. Shops attack, enthusiastic, sound expert in your field. You don't get in those first four seconds, you're done. You can't close anybody. And on that very foundation, the straight line system was built, right? Well, guess what? Turned out, I was actually wrong. A few years back, I'm on the internet looking through some of the studies. I always make sure I keep up on sociology, biology, human communication, right? Harvard University, they do this massive study on justice thing, first impressions in business. Turns out it's not four seconds. Oh, I was so off. It's actually seven seconds. So I'm sorry about that. So up by three seconds. But here's the deal. I think they actually might be wrong, although either way, I've beaten them by almost 30 years, so I still feel pretty good about things. Anyway, whatever the case, here's the weirdest part of all. What they also said in that study is that besides the fact that you have only seven seconds to make that all-important first impression, if you don't make the correct one, it takes seven subsequent impressions to change someone's mind value. Seven. Well, I don't know about you, how far along you are in your career as a salesperson, but you don't get more than one shot. I got one chance. I got to bring it to the table then. I can't be wrong. Bottom line, sharpest attack, enthusiastic, excellent expert in your field. You got four or seven seconds to do it. So the million dollar question here is how? How do you get that across to someone, whether you're in person or on the phone? Let's handle the phone first here. How do you convince someone that your sharpest attack, enthusiastic, excellent expert in your field? Think about it. Is it through the words you say? Think about it for a moment. What could you possibly say to someone to try to get all that across in the first four seconds of a conversation? You have to be literally yelling it, hey, Bill, I'm sharpest attack. I'm enthusiastic. I'm an expert. I'm an expert. God, you're like, you're a freaking idiot, right? Even if it all weren't true, no one would believe you anyway. The simple fact is that the right words to do that, they just don't exist. There's no combination that's profound enough, that's stealthy enough to sneak past the logic center of your prospect's mind and create an emotional reaction right in their gut. Therein lies the key. It's in your prospect's gut. That's where first impressions get formed and it happens in a matter of seconds. By the way, once one gets formed, your prospect will rely on it for all his or her decisions until you say something or do something to prove them wrong. In other words, let's say you made a great first impression, but then 15 seconds later, you say something totally stupid that makes it totally obvious to your prospect that their initial perception of you was wrong. Then what happens at that point is their conscious mind will take over and basically override their unconscious mind's initial perception of you and you'll lose the benefit of that early positioning as an expert. But I'm jumping ahead now. I cover this whole subject of how you take this initial perception and lock it impermanently at the highest level in a later module. So let's say you're at a point here, which is that if it's not your words that create that all-important first impression, then what is it? Well, the answer is actually simple. On the phone, it's through your tone of voice. Specifically, how you say what you say has a profound impact on how it's perceived. And for that matter, how you're perceived. And not just through those first four seconds, but throughout the entire conversation as well. So you have to millions of years of evolution, the human ears become so adept at recognizing tonal shifts, even slight ones, and have a dramatic impact on the meaning or phrase that we term. For example, I remember the greatest thing ever. My wife was raising Bo and my stepson, she was unbelievable with both her tonality and her body language. She could say to him, you know, Bo in. And with that one word, Bo knew he was in trouble by the tone of her voice and her body language. And conversely, she could also look up and say, Bo in. In a nice, sing song tone, with a happier expression. And Bo knew that his mom was just looking for him. Sing word, very different meaning. Now on the flip side of this equation, if the sale takes place in person, then a second form of communication comes into play to work hand in hand with tonality to help you get your point across. We refer to this as body language. Now together, tonality and body language comprise the dual linchpins of what we call unconscious communication. And they play a massive role in how we get a point across. Both while we're talking and while we're listening, your tone of voice, how you move your body, the facial expressions you make, the way you make eye contact, all those little friendly grunts and groans you make while you're listening to someone else talk, those ooze, ahs and the ahs and yups. They're all an integral part of human communication and have a massive impact on how you're perceived. So let me ask you this question here. What percentage of overall communication do you think is comprised of words versus tonality versus body language? What do you think it is? You think 10% is the words? 25%, 50%, 90%? Choose which percentage is made up of words. Close but not the right answer. Try again. That seems like a reasonable guess, but it's not the right answer. Try again. Nope, you're way too high here. Try again. Boom, you got it. Words make up only 10%. In point of fact, tonality and body language together make approximately 90% of our overall communication split about evenly down the middle with each side having approximately 45%, depending on which study you buy into and there's tons of them out there more than you can count. The remaining 10% is comprised of all words, meaning the actual words we say as we verbally communicate. Now, that being said, if you're like most people I speak to or train, I know what you're probably thinking right now. You're like saying, join 10% is the words? That sounds like way too low a number to measure the importance of the words we say, especially in a situation where I'm trying to sell someone something. In fact, if you think back to what Tom and you were on the receiving end, when someone's trying to sell to you, you probably remember you were hanging on the salesperson's every word and judging each one of them to try to find out what they meant. It was if your logical arms almost did hyperdrive, being hyper-visual to try to find out what someone meant with each word from a logical perspective. Right? So, how could it be that only 10% of what we say is the actual words? Tough concept to buy into, right? Well, guess what? Here's the deal. Or should I say the irony? You've actually misinterpreted the meaning of my words. See, despite comprising only 10% of our overall communication, words aren't just important. They're actually the single most important element of our communication strategy. They're only, and this is only true when we finally open up our mouths to speak. In other words, 90% of the time, we're communicating without actually speaking. Get it? Yet in spite of that, when I walk into any phone room or from observing a sales force out in the field, with the exception of maybe one or two salespeople that have naturally their blessed with perfect and flawless tonality and body language, the rest of them are completely missing the boat. And in consequence, they don't get perceived as having even close to the level of expertise that would prompt a prospect to essentially hand over control of the sale and let themselves be guided. And just like that, through this process of basically unwitting self-sabotage, the sale has been poisoned from the very start, making it only a matter of time until it completely spirals out of control. You get this? I'm going to slow down here because this is such a crucial point. In other words, what was happening to the Stratonites and why Danny and I were immune from that is because we had this certain way of sounding, of talking, where we carried ourselves that in those first few seconds, everyone we spoke would say, you know, wow, this person sounds different than not the average bear. They're experts. They're sharp. Something in the way we spoke our tonality, our body language in person. And because of that, they would defer and hand control of the sale. Now, take me at one step further. Remember, every sale can be the same, but only if what? If you're in control. See, if you're not in control, guess what? Yeah, that every sale is in fact different. Every sale can be the same when you are in control and then you slowly but surely and strategically line up every other boom, boom, boom as you move your prospect down the straight line from the open to the close and you do it the same exact way every time. That's power. Now I want to stop here for a few moments while you take a quiz, but please hurry up, finish out this quiz and the exercises and as soon as you can meet me back in the next lesson and we'll rock on. I'll see you there.
